title: What's the Difference Between Web Design and App Design?
excerpt: Web design and app design are related disciplines, but they're not the same — and confusing them can lead to poor hiring decisions and worse products. This post explains the key differences in purpose, process, and expertise between the two.
content:
At first glance, web design and app design seem like the same thing — after all, both involve creating digital interfaces. But they're built on different platforms, designed for different interactions, and require different specialized skills.

Here's what sets them apart.

Platform and Environment

Web design creates interfaces that live in web browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox). The experience must work across a wide range of devices and screen sizes, from large desktop monitors to small phone screens. App design creates interfaces that live as native apps on iOS or Android devices. These apps interact directly with the operating system and hardware — the camera, GPS, biometrics, notifications, and more.

Interaction Models

Web design is primarily designed for mouse and keyboard interaction (with responsive adaptations for touch). App design is built around touch interactions — tapping, swiping, pinching, and scrolling with a finger.

This sounds like a small difference, but it fundamentally changes how interfaces are laid out, how navigation works, and how content is prioritized.

Design Guidelines

Web designers follow general UX best practices and browser standards. App designers must also follow platform-specific guidelines:

  • iOS: Apple's Human Interface Guidelines (HIG)

  • Android: Google's Material Design System


Violating these guidelines can result in app store rejection or a poor user experience.

Discovery and Shareability

Web pages are discoverable via search engines. App screens are not. This changes how content is structured, how pages are labeled, and how SEO factors into design decisions.

Tools Used

Both disciplines often use Figma, but the specifics differ. App designers work with device-specific artboards, auto-layout for mobile viewports, and prototype interactions simulating native touch gestures.

A Team Skilled in Both
